data.operatorNotes: Primary front door for any housing or social-services request in Sedgwick County. Phone-based VI-SPDAT screening routes callers to coordinated entry, shelters, and crisis response. Interpreter line provides 150+ language support. Roughly every third caller ends up in a loop because downstream beds or case managers are unavailable and there is no way to hold a live warm-handoff across agencies after hours.

City-funded street outreach that engages unsheltered people in encampments, under bridges, and in public spaces. Can transport to shelters and medical care. Weekday-only coverage is a known gap — evenings, weekends, and severe-weather periods rely on 211 plus Police HOT referrals, which drop off substantially.

Highest-volume walk-in intake site in the system — 80 to 120 unique contacts per weekday. Runs VI-SPDAT on site, connects to ID assistance, employment services, and shelter referrals. Most contacts who show up in the afternoon have already cycled through at least one other partner that day.

Only round-the-clock youth-specific intake point. Accepts self-referral, law-enforcement drop-offs, and agency referrals. Immediate safety assessment followed by a bed assignment when one exists. When CrossRoads is full the default fallback is adult shelters, which youth frequently refuse.

data.operatingAgency: United Way of the Plains Coalition
data.operatorNotes: Newly rebranded (from Multi-Agency Center) on 2025-05-20. Intended as the single community front door for coordinated entry. Today it handles intake and VI-SPDAT assessments; the automated bed-matching layer and HMIS integration are still being stood up, so referrals to shelters continue to be done by phone between partner staff.

Serves families with children only; no unaccompanied youth and no single adults. Phone screening is followed by an in-person interview, with employment readiness evaluated as part of eligibility. Current intake queue typically runs 2-4 weeks, long enough to be effectively blocked for crisis families.

data.operatingAgency: Kansas Dept. for Children and Families
data.operatorNotes: State benefits office for SNAP, TANF, KanCare, and child-welfare intake. Also runs Independent Living enrollment for aging-out foster youth. Weekday-only hours, typical wait 2-4 hours. No warm handoff — once a case manager leaves the counter, the client's next step depends entirely on the client retaining and carrying paperwork between offices.

Emergency rent, utility, and basic-needs financial assistance. Walk-in triage can issue same-day funds for qualifying cases. Also connects to employment and housing resources. Short weekday window (6 hours) means many arrivals cannot be seen the same day and are queued for return visits.
